comments.body: "The elements are returned in no particular order (unless this set is an instance of some class that provides a guarantee)." is what the iterator method for a set says. found here
A List is not a Set (it doesn't guarantee uniqueness of membership).
In many business unique cases List can't be used just to retain order instead of Set.  LinkedHashSet maintains order and stores unique.

answers.body:
LinkedHashSet is what you need, as it is a Set with a well-defined encounter order.

If the item uniqueness constraint isn't necessary, you could alternatively use a List instead of a Set.

Set is just an interface. In order to retain order, you have to use a specific implementation of that interface and the sub-interface SortedSet, for example TreeSet or LinkedHashSet. You can wrap your Set this way:

Set myOrderedSet = new LinkedHashSet(mySet);

From the javadoc for Set.iterator():

Returns an iterator over the elements in this set. The elements are returned in no particular order (unless this set is an instance of some class that provides a guarantee).

And, as already stated by shuuchan, a TreeSet is an implemention of Set that has a guaranteed order:

The elements are ordered using their natural ordering, or by a Comparator provided at set creation time, depending on which constructor is used.

comments.body: Furthermore, due to the different String hashing in Java 8, the default (unsorted) ordering in Sets and Maps will change. If you rely on unsorted ordering, your code will behave differently under Java 8.

I get that the class not ordering is normal, but the behavior I was expecting was to leave them as they were introduced and not mess with the order, instead it is just shuffling the elements each time one is aggregated. Your solution is not optimal wither because then I will have to implement a whole structure for them to be sorted THE SAME WAY they were introduced :S

@White_King: A set is a mathematical concept that does not contain the notion of "insertion order" so it makes sense for the Java interface to follow its conventions. There are ordered sets but the order is specified by a relation (comparator in Java), again matching the definition in set theory with the definition in Java. Your expectation of it keeping insertion order probably comes from lists but sets are not lists.
As of Java 21, both SortedSet and LinkedHashSet extend/implement SequencedSet, which represents a reversible set with a well-defined element encounter order: stackoverflow.com/a/76460236/1108305.

answers.body:
The Set interface does not provide any ordering guarantees.

Its sub-interface SortedSet, and later NavigableSet, represents a set that is sorted according to some criterion. In Java 6, there are two standard containers that implement SortedSet. They are TreeSet and ConcurrentSkipListSet.

In addition to the SortedSet/NavigableSet interfaces, there is also the LinkedHashSet class. It remembers the order in which the elements were inserted into the set, and returns its elements in that order.

In Java 21+, with sequenced collections added, we have the super-interface of SequencedSet to cover all three of those mentioned classes: ConcurrentSkipListSet, LinkedHashSet, TreeSet.
Normally set does not keep the order, such as HashSet in order to quickly find a emelent, but you can try LinkedHashSet it will keep the order which you put in.
The Set interface itself does not stipulate any particular order. The SortedSet does however.

comments.body: I need a Set not a List, I need a Set that ALSO retains the order of injection of the objects I guess

answers.body: To retain the order use List or a LinkedHashSet.
Iterator returned by Set is not suppose to return data in Ordered way. See this Two java.util.Iterators to the same collection: do they have to return elements in the same order?

comments.body: The question is about retaining the insertion order (which happens to be sorted).

answers.body: Only SortedSet can do the ordering of the Set

Here is a quick summary of the order characteristics of the standard Set implementations available in Java:

  1. keep the insertion order: LinkedHashSet and CopyOnWriteArraySet (thread-safe)
  2. keep the items sorted within the set: TreeSet, EnumSet (specific to enums) and ConcurrentSkipListSet (thread-safe)
  3. does not keep the items in any specific order: HashSet (the one you tried)

For your specific case, you can either sort the items first and then use any of 1 or 2 (most likely LinkedHashSet or TreeSet). Or alternatively and more efficiently, you can just add unsorted data to a TreeSet which will take care of the sorting automatically for you.

As many of the members suggested use LinkedHashSet to retain the order of the collection. U can wrap your set using this implementation.

SortedSet implementation can be used for sorted order but for your purpose use LinkedHashSet.

Also from the docs,

"This implementation spares its clients from the unspecified, generally chaotic ordering provided by HashSet, without incurring the increased cost associated with TreeSet. It can be used to produce a copy of a set that has the same order as the original, regardless of the original set's implementation:"

Source : http://docs.oracle.com/javase/6/docs/api/java/util/LinkedHashSet.html

There are 2 different things.

  1. Sort the elements in a set. For which we have SortedSet and similar implementations.
  2. Maintain insertion order in a set. For which LinkedHashSet and CopyOnWriteArraySet (thread-safe) can be used.

An implementation of interface SequencedSet (introduced in Java 21) can be used when both encounter order and Set semantics (e.g. element uniqueness) are important.

The Set interface does not require a well-defined encounter order, a fact which is explicitly called out in the Javadocs for its iterator method:

Returns an iterator over the elements in this set. The elements are returned in no particular order (unless this set is an instance of some class that provides a guarantee).

The SequencedSet interface is a subinterface of Set that also requires that the implementation have a well-defined encounter order. Both LinkedHashSet and SortedSet implement/extend this new interface.

tl;dr

👉🏽 The method should return SequencedCollection, SequencedSet, or NavigableSet rather than Set.

Update for 2023 and later: Sequenced Collections

You said:

Does a Java Set retain order?

Maybe, maybe not.

The Set interface makes no promises about the encounter order when iterating through the elements of a Set.

The one exception is for a Set returned by either of the Set.of and Set.copyOf static factory methods. These return an unmodifiable Set object whose iteration order of elements is unspecified and is subject to change (each time you iterate, you may get a different encounter order).

You said:

A method is returning a Set to me and supposedly the data is ordered but iterating over the Set, the data is unordered.

As stated above, this behavior is correct. If a method returns a Set, you cannot expect any particular order to the elements encountered. You may even encounter the elements in a different order each time you iterate.

Do not expect what is not promised.

You said:

Is there a better way to manage this?

Yes. Read on.

Does the method need to be changed to return something other than a Set?

Bingo! If a method is promising elements in a particular order when iterating, then that method should not return a Set.

Interface

A method promising a set of elements in a particular encounter order should return an object implementing one or more of these interfaces:

  • SequencedCollection
  • SequencedSet
  • NavigableSet (or its predecessor, SortedSet)

👉🏽 One of these interfaces should be the return type of your method returning a set with a particular encounter order.

SequencedSet < Person > persons = this.fetchPersonsSortedByLastName() ;

The NavigableSet interface has the most features.

The more general super-interface of NavigableSet is SequencedSet. Even more general is SequencedCollection where we lose the distinction of being a Set such as no-duplicates-allowed.

How to choose the interface?

  • If you only need the notion of first/last & reversed, then choose SequencedSet.
  • If you need the features of subsets, floor/ceiling, etc., then use NavigableSet.

Implementation

As for choosing a concrete class, consider:

  • TreeSet and ConcurrentSkipListSet implement all those interfaces. For general purposes, TreeSet is commonly used. If you have a very large number of elements, or need concurrent access, choose the other, ConcurrentSkipListSet. See Skip List in Wikipedia.
  • LinkedHashSet keeps elements in their insertion-order.
  • For a limited set of values known at compile-time, define an enum. Then use an EnumSet to keep the objects in the order in which they are declared within the enum. EnumSet does not implement any of the ordered set interfaces. But if an enum suits your domain, then I would recommend EnumSet as it is extremely efficient both with CPU and with memory. You will have to return the set as either Set or EnumSet rather than the interfaces discussed above.
Concrete class Order Collection
& Set		 SequencedCollection
& SequencedSet		 NavigableSet
& SortedSet
TreeSet &
ConcurrentSkipListSet		 Natural order
or Comparator		 ✅ ✅ ✅
LinkedHashSet Insertion order ✅ ✅ ❌
EnumSet Declared enum order ✅ ❌ ❌

And of course there may be third-party implementations of interest to you. Be aware of the libraries Google Guava and Eclipse Collections.

Example code

Let's practice with this simple record class.

record Person( String firstName , String lastName ) { }

Create a method that returns a SequencedSet. Here we use TreeSet as our choice of implementation of that interface. We are free to later change that implementation to ConcurrentSkipListSet without breaking any calling code as both concrete classes implement the interface SequencedSet.

Notice how we start with a list of Person objects in arbitrary order. We pass that list to the addAll method of TreeSet, a SequencedSet implementation. The TreeSet automatically sorts the elements per the Comparator we specify.

 SequencedSet < Person > fetchPersonsSortedByLastName ( )
{
    List < Person > personList =
            List.of (
                    new Person ( "Carol" , "Cooper" ) ,
                    new Person ( "Alice" , "Anderson" ) ,
                    new Person ( "Davis" , "Danielson" ) ,
                    new Person ( "Bob" , "Barker" )
            );
    SequencedSet < Person > personsByLastName = new TreeSet <> ( Comparator.comparing ( Person :: lastName ).thenComparing ( Person :: firstName ) );
    personsByLastName.addAll ( personList );
    return personsByLastName;
}

Exercise that method.

SequencedSet < Person > persons = this.fetchPersonsSortedByLastName ( );
persons.forEach ( System.out :: println );

As a result, we see the elements iterate in sorted order.

Person[firstName=Alice, lastName=Anderson]
Person[firstName=Bob, lastName=Barker]
Person[firstName=Carol, lastName=Cooper]
Person[firstName=Davis, lastName=Danielson]

If your intention is to maintain an encounter order, do not choose HashSet or CopyOnWriteArraySet.

Sequenced collections were added to Java 21 and later. See JEP 431: Sequenced Collections. And see the talk, Sequenced Collections, by Stuart Marks of 2023-10.

title: Java Set retain order?
body: Does a Java Set retain order? A method is returning a Set to me and supposedly the data is ordered but iterating over the Set, the data is unordered. Is there a better way to manage this? Does the method need to be changed to return something other than a Set?
